title: What Is Your AI Tutor Doing?

modules.title: Meet Mira: Your New Study Buddy
modules.sub: Step inside an AI tutor and see how it thinks
modules.description: Learners log into the fictional platform MIRA and experience a tutoring session from the student's seat, noticing what the AI says, skips, and emphasises. They begin keeping a observation log of MIRA's choices to fuel investigations in later modules.

modules.title: Why Does It Get Some Things Wrong?
modules.sub: Probe the cracks and catch MIRA making mistakes
modules.description: Learners deliberately try to trip MIRA up — asking ambiguous questions, giving wrong answers, and pushing edge cases — to surface real failure modes like confident errors and unhelpful hints. They classify each failure and form a hypothesis about why the system behaved that way.

modules.title: Design a Better Hint System
modules.sub: Redesign one feature to make MIRA more honest
modules.description: Working from their failure logs, learners sketch and justify a redesigned hint feature that signals uncertainty or admits when MIRA doesn't know something. They compare their design choices with real systems like Khanmigo and explain the trade-offs they made.

modules.title: Teach the Tutor, Test Yourself
modules.sub: Flip roles — become the designer, quiz the system
modules.description: Learners write a short set of rules that an ideal AI tutor should follow, then stress-test those rules by playing the tutor themselves with a partner acting as student. They reflect on what they now understand about how AI tutoring systems shape learning and where human judgement still matters.

synopsis: You step inside the fictional AI tutoring platform MIRA and play both student and designer — experiencing how an AI tutor decides what to say, what to skip, and when to encourage you. By reverse-engineering its choices, you learn what real systems like Khanmigo are actually doing beneath the surface.
rationale: AI tutors are being adopted in classrooms globally while most students treat them as magic, leaving them unable to identify when the tutor is confidently wrong or subtly steering their thinking.
learning_outcome: Students can explain at least two ways an AI tutoring system shapes learning, identify one failure mode from personal testing, and redesign one feature to make the tutor more honest about its limits.
source_signals: Meet Khanmigo: the student tutor AI being tested in school districts | 60 Minutes - CBS News
Comparing Online and AI-Assisted Learning: A Student's View - Education Next
Project Overview ‹ Sparki: An Interactive Learning Companion for AI Education - MIT Media Lab
